abstract.t: Verifiable Convergence Protocol (VCP) defines the minimal data types and APIs required for autonomous agents and human-machine systems to achieve epistemic convergence without centralized truth arbiters. VCP is transport-agnostic, blockchain-agnostic, and model-agnostic. Any system implementing VCP is an Epistemic Execution System (EES).
The key invariant: C(manipulation) > C(validation) for all Claim objects.

section.title: Introduction
section.name: Introduction
section.t: As artificial intelligence transitions to autonomous agents capable of independent actions, the lack of a standardized mechanism for verifiable trust and epistemic convergence has become a critical bottleneck. VCP addresses this by providing a minimal protocol for claims, actions, and verifiable traces.
The key invariant is that the cost of manipulating a Claim's confidence exceeds the cost of validating it for all Claim objects: C(manipulation) > C(validation).

section.title: Conformance
section.name: Conformance
section.t: A system is VCP-Compatible iff:

ol.li: It implements the 4 Core Endpoints in Section 4.
confidence for all Claims is computed via Multiplicative Weights Update + Gossip Consensus as defined in Section 5 (GQRVP).
All Action objects reference Claim[] as basis. Actions without basis MUST be rejected at Gateway.
Trace is immutable, content-addressed, and publicly auditable.

section.title: Claim
section.name: Claim
section.t: The atomic unit of verifiable information.

section.title: Formal Completeness Boundary (Gödel-Grounded)
section.name: Formal Completeness Boundary (Gödel-Grounded)
section.t: The Verifiable Convergence Protocol operates at the mathematical boundary of formal provability defined by Kurt Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem when applied to distributed consensus.
The protocol defines a formal-to-epistemic transition threshold:

ul.li: When confidence is at or above the threshold, the system may treat the result as formally sufficient.
When confidence falls below the threshold, the protocol SHOULD escalate to Continuum-style semantic verification.

section.t: Empirically, the sybil-10x-g1.5 trace indicates that a substantial majority of convergence outcomes exceed the threshold, while the remainder fall into the regime where formal methods alone are insufficient. This is consistent with the intended Gödel-grounded design: no formal system can resolve all cases with certainty, and the remainder must be handled by epistemic verification (see [FOUNDATIONS]).

section.title: Attestation
section.name: Attestation
section.t: Cryptographic signature over a Claim by a DID.

section.title: Action
section.name: Action
section.t: A proposed state change that MUST be grounded in Claims.

section.title: Trace
section.name: Trace
section.t: Immutable record of verification.

section.title: Core Endpoints
section.name: Core Endpoints
section.t: All endpoints MUST be served over HTTPS. Request/response bodies are application/json.

section.title: POST /v1/claim
section.name: POST /v1/claim
section.t: Submit a new Claim for verification.
Request: Claim object without claim_id, confidence.
Response 201: {"claim_id": "sha256:..."}
Response 400: Invalid Claim schema.

section.title: GET /v1/claim/{claim_id}
section.name: GET /v1/claim/{claim_id}
section.t: Retrieve a Claim and its current network confidence.
Response 200:
Response 404: Claim not found.

section.title: POST /v1/action/verify
section.name: POST /v1/action/verify
section.t: Verify if an Action is permitted given its basis Claims.
Request: Action object.
Response 200:
Gateway MUST reject if basis is empty or any claim_id has confidence < 0.7.

section.title: GET /v1/trace/{trace_id}
section.name: GET /v1/trace/{trace_id}
section.t: Retrieve immutable verification history for an Action.
Response 200: Trace object.
Response 404: Trace not found.

section.title: GQRVP: Security Parameters
section.name: GQRVP: Security Parameters
section.t: To prevent Sybil and amplification attacks, VCP-Compatible implementations MUST use the following parameters for the Grover Amplification Loop:

tr.td: Controls weight update speed. Prevents flip-flopping.
Exponent for global score. Amplifies consensus, dampens noise.
Gossip mix ratio. Prevents Sybil cluster isolation.

section.t: A formal proof of 67% Byzantine resilience under these parameters is given in /specs/RFC/GQRVP-security.md.

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.title: Security Considerations
section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: VCP does not resolve conflicts caused by asymmetry of goals. VCP guarantees that the cost of manipulating a Claim's confidence exceeds the cost of validating it, given an honest supermajority of stake-weighted attention.

front.title: Gravit Epistemic Foundations: Gödel Incompleteness in Distributed Reasoning

section.title: Appendix B: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem and theta_critical
section.name: Appendix B: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorem and theta_critical
section.t: The threshold theta_critical = 0.731 is introduced as a practical boundary between formal sufficiency and semantic escalation. This value is grounded in the theoretical observation that no formal system can prove all true statements of interest in a sufficiently expressive setting, as established by Gödel's Second Incompleteness Theorem (see [FOUNDATIONS]).
In the sybil-10x-g1.5 empirical trace, the analysis produced the following summary:

ul.li: Threshold analysis: 0 scores below threshold, 30 above threshold

Interpretation: the trace demonstrates a stable regime where high-confidence convergence dominates, while the protocol still reserves a formal-to-epistemic transition point for lower-confidence cases.

section.t: This appendix is intended as an RFC-ready rationale for the threshold and for the requirement to escalate unresolved cases to semantic verification via Continuum.
